coredata.dc:title: One-step facile green synthesis of a highly fluorescent molecule through a way towards carbon dots and detection of dopamine based on in-situ formation of silver nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Shenghong
Sun, Xiaohan
Wang, Zhaoyan
Wang, Xiayan
Guo, Guangsheng
Pu, Qiaosheng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots derived from citric acid and amines have been gaining considerable attention due to their high quantum efficiency, but there are researches proven the presence of small fluorescent molecules in those materials. In this work, with proper control of the synthetic conditions, highly fluorescent 5-oxo-3,5-dihydro-2H-thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (TPCA) as nice needle-like crystals were harvested by a simple one-step hydrothermal method directly based on the reaction of citric acid and cysteamine hydrochloride. The proposed synthesis route does not need extra complicated purification process. The yield of TPCA was 46.9% and the quantum yield was as high as 83.2%. Experimental results confirmed that TPCA possesses all advantages of carbon dots reported previously with similar starting materials. In addition, a novel and effective fluorescent probe, TPCA-Ag+, was designed for sensitive and selective sensing of dopamine (DA) based on in-situ formation of sliver nanoparticles. The calibration curve for DA was linear up to 0.4μmol/L, and a detection limit of 0.75 nmol/L was attained. The method was successfully applied to the determination of DA in dopamine hydrochloride injection and urine samples and the standard addition recoveries were in a range of 87.2–102.8%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Fluorescent molecule
TPCA
Sensing
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of DA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of TPCA by one-step hydrothermal method from citric acid and cysteamine hydrochloride (a). Schematic illustration of the sensing system for the detection of DA (b).
Optical photo of TPCA after dried (a), SEM image of synthesized TPCA (b). Inset: the optical photograph of TPCA in polytetrafluoroethylene reactor after hydrothermal reaction finished.
H1NMR (a) and C1NMR (b) spectrum of TPCA.

The UV–vis and PL spectra of TPCA aqueous solution (a), PL spectra of TPCA at different excitation wavelength (b). Inset: the optical photo of TPCA solution under natural light (left) and 365nm UV light (right).
UV–vis and PL spectra of TPCA, TPCA-DA, TPCA-Ag+ and TPCA-Ag+-DA.
Quenching of the fluorescence of TPCA-Ag+ with different concentrations of DA (a). Plots of ΔI/I0 vs the concentration of DA (b).

Selectivity of the TPCA-Ag+-based sensor for DA. DA, 10μmol/L; other compounds, 50μmol/L (DA, dopamine; AA, ascorbic acid; UA, uric acid; Thr, threonine; Ser, serine; Phe, phenylalanine; Lys, lysine; l-Cys, l-cysteine; Arg, arginine; His, histidine; BSA, bovine serum albumin; Glu, glucose; Fru, fructose; Lac, lactose).
Determination of DA in dopamine injection and urine samples.

head.ce:title: One-step facile green synthesis of a highly fluorescent molecule through a way towards carbon dots and detection of dopamine based on in-situ formation of silver nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metals Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, Department of Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, Gansu 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metals Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of pharmacy Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, Gansu, 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory for Green Catalysis and Separation, Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing, 100124, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory for Green Catalysis and Separation
Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots derived from citric acid and amines have been gaining considerable attention due to their high quantum efficiency, but there are researches proven the presence of small fluorescent molecules in those materials. In this work, with proper control of the synthetic conditions, highly fluorescent 5-oxo-3,5-dihydro-2H-thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (TPCA) as nice needle-like crystals were harvested by a simple one-step hydrothermal method directly based on the reaction of citric acid and cysteamine hydrochloride. The proposed synthesis route does not need extra complicated purification process. The yield of TPCA was 46.9% and the quantum yield was as high as 83.2%. Experimental results confirmed that TPCA possesses all advantages of carbon dots reported previously with similar starting materials. In addition, a novel and effective fluorescent probe, TPCA-Ag+, was designed for sensitive and selective sensing of dopamine (DA) based on in-situ formation of sliver nanoparticles. The calibration curve for DA was linear up to 0.4μmol/L, and a detection limit of 0.75 nmol/L was attained. The method was successfully applied to the determination of DA in dopamine hydrochloride injection and urine samples and the standard addition recoveries were in a range of 87.2–102.8%.

ce:section.ce:para: Broad application of fluorescence has inspired long-lasting endeavor on the exploring of fluorescent materials [1–4]. Recently, fluorescent probes synthesized from simple starting materials have attracted great attention mainly because of the low-cost of preparation and the high quantum efficiency they can provide [5]. Citric acid is one of such starting materials for highly fluorescent materials due to the presence of three carboxyl groups and one hydroxyl group. As a typical example, Olthoff et al. found that citric acid was able to form fluorescent derivatives with D-penicillamine [6]. In recent years, synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) through pyrolysis of citric acid and various amine-based surface passivation agents has been extensively reported [7]. A widely adopted protocol for highly fluorescent CDs is based on the reaction of citric acid with β-amine derivatives, such as ethylenediamine [8], ethanolamine [9] and cysteine [10]. The preparation process and optical properties of these products are roughly similar. The most significant feature of this protocol is the excellent optical property of the products, a quantum yield (QY) of 80% has been achieved at an excitation wavelength around 360nm. However, the mechanism of their ultrahigh QY is still ambiguous, there is evidence of the presence of a class of fluorescent molecules with similar quantum efficiency to CDs after careful purification [11]. Kasprzyk et al. reported that these types of fluorescent molecules could be successfully applied in the fabrication of a set of biodegradable photoluminescent polymers [12]. Due to the high cellular biocompatibility, minimal inflammatory response in vivo, excellent photostability and controlled degradability, biodegradable photoluminescent polymers could be used for optical element building, bioimaging and tissue regeneration [13–15]. Similarly, these fluorescent molecules could also be embedded or grafted to nanomaterials and form nanocomposites for better use. However, inconvenient synthesis and purification processes of these fluorescent molecules limit their application. Kasprzyk et al. have demonstrated that some fluorescent molecules could be prepared through standard organic synthesis, but their preparation process normally contains several steps and the products needed to be purified by column chromatography and organic solvents were used. The yield of the target fluorescent molecules was relatively low (10%–20%) [16]. Therefore, it deserves to develop a simple and eco-friendly approach to synthesize these types of fluorescent molecules for better application.

Dopamine (DA), as a neurotransmitter, plays a critical role in mammalian central nervous system and can influence learning, memory, various motivated behaviors, attentiveness, and neuronal plasticity [17,18]. Some nervous system diseases, including schizophrenia, epilepsy, senile dementia and Parkinson disease, have been found to be associated with abnormal levels of dopamine [19–21]. Therefore, fast and reliable detection of trace DA has attracted considerable attentions [22]. Up to now, various analytical techniques including electrochemical methods [23,24], high performance liquid chromatography [25], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [26], chemiluminescence [27,28], and capillary/chip electrophoresis [29,30], just name a few, have been applied in the detection of DA. Among those methods, electrochemical routes are overwhelming in recent years due to the electroactive nature of DA [31]. Although the electrochemical approach possesses good sensitivity, its selectivity is limited by the interference from molecules with similar red-ox behavior to DA, particularly ascorbic acid and uric acid which could be oxidized at potentials close to that of DA [32]. Attractively, fluorescence detection based on organic fluorophores has been proved to be a sensitive and cost-effective method. It is also suitable to be incorporated into portable devices for on-site testing and has been widely explored in recent years [33,34]. However, the preparation of target organic fluorophores normally requires multiple operation steps and tedious purifying process. Hence, it is necessary to develop simple and efficient synthesis route of organic fluorophores and design new detection systems to meet the increasing demand of the detection of DA with high sensitivity and selectivity.

Herein, we show that through proper selection of the reactants and control of the synthetic conditions, a citric acid based fluorescent molecule, 5-oxo-3,5-dihydro-2H-thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (TPCA) can be obtained directly by an one-step facile green hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 a). The precursor solution of citric acid and cysteamine hydrochloride was subject directly to facile hydrothermal treatment. Pure TPCA with nice crystal structure can be harvested without any further purification; the yield can reach 46.9%. TPCA emits stable bright blue fluorescence with a QY of 83.2%. The characteristic of DA to reduce Ag+ to Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs) in-situ [35] was utilized together with the unique UV–vis absorption of AgNPs to tactfully build an effective fluorescent probe (TPCA-Ag+) for the sensitive and selective detection of DA (Scheme 1b). Its applicability was verified by quantifying of spiked DA in real samples. The main advantages of the proposed method include green synthesis of TPCA through the one-step hydrothermal route, and the sensitive and selective detection of DA in real samples.

Citric acid monohydrate was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). Cysteamine hydrochloride, dopamine (DA) hydrochloride and silver nitrate were used as received from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide was provided by Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All reagents were analytical pure without further purification. The deionized water (>18MΩ) used throughout the work was obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, Bedford MA).

The UV–vis absorption spectra and the fluorescence spectra were measured at room temperature on a UV 2800SPC spectrophotometer (Shanghai Sunny Hengping Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) and a F97Pro fluorospectrophotometer (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China), respectively. The morphology of product was characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM) on JSM-5600LV (JEOL, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded on a Nicolet NEXUS 670 spectrometer. The mass spectrometry (MS) was recorded on Esquire 6000. The nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) analysis was performed on a JNM-ECS (JEOL, Japan). The single crystal X-ray diffraction was recorded on a SuperNova system (Agilent, USA).

The typical procedure is described as follows: 1g citric acid monohydrate and 1.08g cysteamine hydrochloride were dissolved in 5mL water. Then, the resulted mixture was heated hydrothermally in a 50mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 180°C for 3h. After cooled down to room temperature, the product was vacuum filtered and washed twice with deionized water and ethanol, then dried naturally, yellow needle crystal can be obtained without extra purification steps, which is verified to be TPCA.

QY was determined by the comparative method. Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (QY of 54%) was used as the reference. QYs were calculated based on the following equation:
QYx
= QYQuin (IMat/AMat)(AQuin/IQuin)(ηMat/ηQuin)2
where Ι is the integrated area of the emission intensity ranging from 380 to 700nm, and Α is the absorbance at 350nm, and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript ‘Mat' refers to the measured material; ‘Quin' refers to quinine sulfate. Five different concentrations were measured for each material and the reported was the average value. Absorbance of all solutions was kept under 0.1 for the measurement.

The TPCA and AgNO3 were dissolved in phosphate buffer (0.1M, pH 8.5) as the stock solution (TPCA, 50μmol/L; Ag+, 10μmol/L) which is referred to as TPCA-Ag+. As a typical procedure, 10μL of DA was added to 3mL of prepared TPCA-Ag+ solution and incubated at room temperature for 5min. Then, the fluorescent emission was recorded at 420nm with excitation wavelength at 350nm. The experiment conditions including pH, incubation time and the concentration of silver ions and TPCA were all investigated and the optimized values were used for the measurement. Calibration curves were prepared by using standards dissolved in deionized water.

Dopamine hydrochloride injection samples were obtained from Jiangsu Yabang Johnson & Johnson pharmaceutical Co., LTD. and no extra pretreatment was performed except a dilution using phosphate buffer (0.1M, pH 8.5) before analysis. A urine sample was provided by a female volunteer. The sample was centrifuged for 10min (12,000rpm) in order to precipitate the proteins. Then it was filtered and the filtrate was diluted 100 times using phosphate buffer (0.1M, pH 8.5) before analysis.

Through a systematic selection of several commonly available β-amine derivatives, a highly fluorescent material was obtained using citric acid and cysteamine hydrochloride by a one-step hydrothermal method (Scheme 1a). As shown in Fig. 1 a, the products are yellow needle-like crystal with a yield of 46.9%, which is much higher than that reported previously [16]. The crystals could be seen in the autoclave immediately after the reaction mixture was cooled down (inset of Fig. 1a). And the SEM image clearly indicates the morphology of the crystal (Fig. 1b). The crystals are good enough for X-ray single crystal diffraction analysis without further re-crystallization. The results demonstrated that the crystal was 5-oxo-3,5-dihydro-2H-thiazolo[3,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (TPCA, Fig. 2 ). In addition, MS (Fig. S1), 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. 3 a) and 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. 3b) all support the structure of the compound. As shown in Fig. S2, the formation mechanism of the compound should be similar to that proposed by Olthoff et al. previously [6]. In particular, if cysteamine, rather than cysteamine hydrochloride was used as the starting material, we only got brown solution without crystal (see Fig. S3), so chloride ions may play an important role in the formation of TPCA crystals.

The structure and composition of TPCA was further verified with FTIR (Fig. S4). Characteristic stretching vibration of υ (OH) and υ (CO) at 3418.2cm−1 and 1714.6cm−1 can be observed. The absorption peaks at 1641.9cm−1 and 1253.5cm−1 can be assigned to carbonyl groups and υ(CN). The peak at 1520.3cm−1 can be assigned to the framework vibration of υ(CC).

The spectral property of TPCA was studied systemically. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the aqueous TPCA solution (Fig. 4 a) show that the strongest fluorescence emission band locates at 420nm under an excitation of 350nm. The QY was 83.2%, higher than that of CDs prepared with similar starting materials. More importantly, no wavelength dependence was observed (Fig. 4b). The transparent TPCA solution can emit bright blue fluorescence under UV light of 365nm (inset of Fig. 4a). A UV–vis absorption band centered at 345nm (Fig. 4a), it agreed with the excitation spectrum.

The other properties of the TPCA and the factors that might affect its optical performance were also examined. The TPCA could be well dispersed in most of common organic solvents including methanol, ethanol, acetonitrile, acetone, dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), tetrahydrofuran (THF), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), chloroform and diethyl ether, maintained its luminescence (Fig. S5). It may be conducive to fabricate photovoltaic cells in organic phases. The stability of TPCA was also studied, the luminescent emission in aqueous solutions was stable at pH 5–13, and decreased sharply with the increase of acidity accompanied with slight red-shift (from 420 to 443nm, Fig. S6). This decrease is likely due to the loss of charge of TPCA under strong acidic condition, which results in the aggregation of TPCA and decrease of PL intensity. To confirm the stability of TPCA under high ionic strength environment, we examined the salt tolerance of TPCA by measuring the PL intensity of TPCA with different concentrations NaCl. As shown in Fig. S7, less than 1% decrease in the PL intensity was observed at a concentration of 1mol/L, which reveals that TPCA are stable in the medium with high ionic strength. As shown in Fig. S8, under continuous illumination of the excitation light (350nm) for 120min in the fluorescence spectrophotometer, less than 5% of decrease in the PL intensity was observed. These results indicated that the photobleaching of TPCA can be negligible for the fast analytical application. In addition, we also investigated effect of various metal ions on the fluorescence of TPCA through the addition of different metal ions (Fe2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Ag+, Cu2+ and Co2+) into TPCA solutions. From Fig. S9, it is obvious that reasonable concentrations of common heavy metal ions cause virtually no change in the emission intensity in aqueous solution. All above results suggest that the fluorescent property of TPCA is stable.

With the aid of the spectral characteristic of sliver nanoparticles (AgNPs), an effective fluorescent probe based on TPCA-Ag+ was established and successfully applied for the sensitive and selective detection of DA. From Fig. 5 a, we can see that Ag+ or DA only cannot induce the change of the fluorescence of TPCA, but small amount of DA added to TPCA-Ag+ solution caused apparently quenching of the fluorescence. The reason behind this phenomenon is that DA possesses strong reducibility and can induce the formation of AgNPs under weakly alkaline condition [35]. Therefore, if the TPCA-Ag+ complex is used as the fluorescent probe, in-situ formed AgNPs may quench the fluorescence of TPCA, the content of DA can be determined accordingly. The mechanism of the sensitive fluorescence response of TPCA-Ag+ toward DA was also confirmed by UV–vis. As shown in Fig. 5b, no visible absorption was observed at 405nm for TPCA-Ag+, but the mixture of TPCA-Ag+ and DA showed strong visible absorption at 405nm and the colour changed from colorless to light yellow (inset of Fig. 5b), indicating the formation of AgNPs. Because the fluorescence emission of TPCA (420nm) locates exactly within the absorption of AgNPs (335 nm–500nm), energy transfer leads to apparently fluorescence quenching. From Fig. 5a, the effect of Ag+ on the PL intensity of TPCA can be ignored. Therefore, in-situ formation of AgNPs is essential for TPCA-Ag+ based determination of DA.

The analytical performance of the proposed fluorescent probe was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 6 , the PL intensity of the TPCA-Ag+ decreased linearly with increasing the concentration of DA. At a TPCA concentration of 50μmol/L, a good linear relationship was achieved in the DA concentration range of 0.005–0.4μmol/L and the correlation coefficient is 0.996 (Fig. 6b). Without sample preconcentration, the limit of detection (LOD) calculated base on 3 times of standard deviation of the blank signal (S/N=3) is 0.75 nmol/L for DA. This value is better than that of electrochemistry (83 nmol/L) [24], chemiluminescence (2.3 nmol/L) [27] and electrophoresis (2.35 nmol/L) [37]. It is comparable with high-performance liquid chromatography with amperometric detection (1.1 nmol/L) [23] and mass spectrometric detection (0.8 nmol/L) [38], but the instruments for the later two are complicated. Compared with fluorescent nanomaterials, for example, graphene quantum dots [39] and CdTe quantum dots [40], which gave LODs of 90 nmol/L and 8 nmol/L, respectively, the sensitivity of proposed fluorescent probe is much higher. Therefore, the TPCA-Ag+ based fluorescent probe is a sensitive and promising choice for the DA detection.

Selectivity is an important parameter to evaluate the performance of the sensing system. The potential co-existing substances that with reducing capability and that may affect the formation of AgNPs were tested, which include ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), sugars, amino acids and bovine serum albumin (BSA), no interference was found for all tested compounds at reasonable concentrations (Fig. 7 ). Based on these results, although reducing capability is necessary for the formation of AgNPs, but there are other factors that may affect the response of TPCA-Ag+. DA can spontaneously form polydopamine under weak alkaline condition, thus the AgNPs can be protected without flocculation and disperse in the water solution stably. Despite AA is a strong reducing agent, the formation of AgNPs through the reduction of AA normally needs other stabilizer, so AA at a reasonable concentration did not interfere with the detection of DA. Same as AA, UA normally causes interference in electrochemical detection of DA but it did not affect the PL intensity. These results indicated that the proposed probe can be used for the selective detection of DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the proposed probe for the determination of DA in real samples was evaluated by analyzing DA in dopamine hydrochloride injection and urine samples. The spiked samples were prepared by addition of DA standards into appropriately diluted dopamine hydrochloride injection and filtrate of urine. As given in Table 1 , the corresponding recovery values for dopamine injection and urine samples ranged from 97.6 to 102.8%, 87.2 to 96.2%, respectively. And the RSDs are within 4.61% and 5.27%, respectively, which confirms the applicability of the method in the real analysis.

In summary, we provided a simple, low-cost and green route for synthesis of highly fluorescent molecule TPCA by using citric acid and cysteamine hydrochloride through hydrothermal treatment. TPCA crystals can be obtained without further purification. The yield of 46.9% can be achieved. Further, a sensitive and specific fluorescence sensing system was established for fast and sensitive determination of DA through a designed TPCA-Ag+ fluorescent probe based on the formation of AgNPs. The method was successfully applied in the detection of DA in dopamine hydrochloride injection and urine samples, indicating that our approach is applicable for the detection of real samples. The most important merit of the proposed method is its good toleration of AA and UA. The detection mechanism based on the in-situ formation of metal nanoparticles from fluorescent complex can extend the application range of fluorescence quenching-based detection. Meanwhile, the TPCA molecule has a carboxyl group and can be easily modified. Due to with excellent biocompatibility TPCA that has been proven by previously research [36], it can be used as a derivative reagent to label proteins and other biomolecules.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21575054, 21625501 and 21527808).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.06.137.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular fluorescent probes for monitoring pH changes in living cells
Cesium carbonate functionalized graphene quantum dots as stable electron-selective layer for improvement of inverted polymer solar cells
Nanostructured sensors for detection of heavy metals: a review
Optical molecular sensing with semiconductor quantum dots (QDs)

Improving the functionality of carbon nanodots: doping and surface functionalization
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
Formation mechanism of carbogenic nanoparticles with dual photoluminescence emission
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
Investigation from chemical structure to photoluminescent mechanism: a type of carbon dots from the pyrolysis of citric acid and an amine
Luminescence phenomena of biodegradable photoluminescent poly(diol citrates)
Biodegradable, elastomeric, and intrinsically photoluminescent poly(silicon-citrates) with high photostability and biocompatibility for tissue regeneration and bioimaging
Fluorescence imaging enabled urethane-doped citrate-based biodegradable elastomers
Poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) as ion-conducting polymer for biodegradable light-emitting electrochemical cells
Novel efficient fluorophores synthesized from citric acid
Recent progress in development of dopamine receptor subtype-selective agents: potential therapeutics for neu-rological and psychiatric disorders
A role for phasic dopamine release within the nucleus accumbens in encoding aversion: a review of the neurochemical literature
The short-latency dopamine signal: a role in discovering novel actions?
Electrical stimuli in the central nervous system microenvironment
Dopamine, schizophrenia, mania, and depression: toward a unified hypothesis of cortico-striatopallido-thalamic function
Electrochemical sensors and biosensors for determination of catecholamine neurotransmitters: a review
Fabrication of CoTiO3-TiO2 composite films from a heterobimetallic single source precursor for electrochemical sensing of dopamine
Simultaneous analysis of dopamine and homovanillic acid by high-performance liquid chromatography with wall-jet/thin-layer electrochemical detection
Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy detection of dopamine by DNA targeting amplification assay in Parkisons's model
A sensitive biosensor for dopamine determination based on the unique catalytic chemiluminescence of metal-organic framework HKUST-1
Near-infrared electrogenerated chemiluminescence of ultrasmall Ag2Se quantum dots for the detection of dopamine
Recent contributions of capillary electrophoresis to neuroscience
Electrochemical detection of dopamine using water-soluble sulfonated graphene
Carbon-based electrodes to improve the electrochemical detection of dopamine
Fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for detection of lead cadmium, and mercury ions
Design and synthesis of fluorescent reagents for selective detection of dopamine
Colorimetric detection of copper ions in tap water during the synthesis of silver/dopamine nanoparticles
Carbon dots with high fluorescence quantum yield: the fluorescence originates from organic fluorophores
Sensitive determination of neurotransmitters in urine by microchip electrophoresis with multiple-concentration approaches combining field-amplified and reversed-field stacking

An optimized method for neurotransmitters and their metabolites analysis in mouse hypothalamus by high performance liquid chromatography-Q Exactive hybrid quadrupole-orbitrap high-resolution accurate mass spectrometry
Graphene quantum dots as effective probes for label-free fluorescence detection of dopamine
Near-infrared excitation of CdTe quantum dots based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer and their use as fluorescent sensors

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shenghong Yang obtained his Ph.D. from Lanzhou University, in 2016. His research interests are the synthesis and functionalization of fluorescence materials and their application in sensors.

Xiaohan Sun has been pursuing her MSc degree at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University since 2014. Her research interests are the synthesis of carbon dots and their application as sensors for the pollutants.

Zhaoyan Wang is an associate professor of School of Pharmacy, Lanzhou University. She received her Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Lanzhou University. Her current research interests include pharmaceutical analysis with microchip electrophoresis/capillary electrophoresis, synthesis of new fluorescent materials and their application.

Xiayan Wang received her Ph.D. from University of Science Technology of China (USTC). She is currently a professor in Beijing University of Technology. She was awarded the National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars from NSFC in 2016. Her research interests include micro/nanoscale analysis, microfluidic synthesis, catalysts for low temperature fuel cells, and materials for sodium ion batteries.

Guangsheng Guo received his Ph.D. from Beijing University of Chemical Technology. He is currently a professor in Beijing University of Technology. He was awarded the 2014 Special Government Allowances of the State Council of China. His research interests include nanotechnology/nanomaterials, microfluidic synthesis, and micro/nanoscale analysis.

Qiaosheng Pu received his Ph.D. from Lanzhou University in 1998. He is currently a professor of College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University. His research interests are mainly on biosensors, microfluidics and their application.
